The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the rest from all enemies — "and the LORD gave them rest round about, according to all that he sware unto their fathers: and there stood not a man of all their enemies before them; the LORD delivered all their enemies into their hand": God gave Israel rest on every side, subduing all their foes; the truth that no foe can stand when God fights for his people, the rest and the conquest his gift, "the LORD your God he it is that fighteth for you," Joshua 23:10; the figure — the rest God gives his people from all their spiritual enemies, the victory of faith, and the eternal rest that remains, "there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God," Hebrews 4:9. The figure of the rest God gives his people from all their foes, the victory wholly his gift.

On "and the LORD gave them rest round about, according to all that he sware unto their fathers," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the rest given. Keil and Ellicott note God giving Israel rest on every side as he had sworn, the rest his gift and the fulfilment of his oath (the rest from enemies, Psalm 44:3) — the rest (round about).

On "and there stood not a man of all their enemies before them; the LORD delivered all their enemies into their hand," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the victory. Calvin and Gill note no enemy able to stand before Israel, all their foes delivered into their hand by the LORD, the victory his and not their own, a figure of the rest that remains and the final triumph (the victory God's gift, Joshua 23:10; Hebrews 4:9) — the figure (the rest from all spiritual enemies, the eternal rest); the providence (the rest, the great summary that no good thing failed to follow, vv.45). The verse displays God giving Israel rest and delivering all their enemies into their hand.

The witnesses agree the substance: God gave Israel rest on every side, and no enemy could stand before them (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the rest from enemies: the LORD gave them rest round about — the complete rest and victory God gave his people, no foe able to stand before them, the victory not by their own sword but by the LORD (Psalm 44:3), God fighting for his people so that no enemy could stand (Joshua 23:10), a figure of the rest God gives from all spiritual enemies and the eternal rest that remains (Hebrews 4:9). The verse displays the rest-from-enemies crux. God giving Israel rest and delivering all their enemies into their hand displays the crux: the LORD gave them rest round about... and there stood not a man of all their enemies before them; the LORD delivered all their enemies into their hand (Psalm 44:3; Joshua 23:10; Hebrews 4:9) — the rest God gives his people from all their foes, the victory wholly his gift. The verse displays the great summary, that not one good thing of all God spake failed (vv.45).
45:
"There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass." The witnesses note the great summary, that not one good thing of all the LORD had spoken to Israel failed, but all came to pass — the crux of the perfect faithfulness of God, every word of his promise fulfilled. On "there failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass," interpreters expound the faithfulness. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the perfect faithfulness of God — "there failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass": not one of all God's good promises to Israel failed, but every one was fulfilled; the truth that the faithfulness of God is the ground of all assurance, his every promise sure and certain, "the word of the LORD is right; and all his works are done in truth," Psalm 33:4; the figure — the certainty of every promise of God, fulfilled in Christ in whom all the promises are yea and amen, "all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him Amen," 2 Corinthians 1:20. The figure of the perfect faithfulness of God, every word of his promise fulfilled.

On "there failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note nothing failing. Keil and Ellicott note that not one of God's good words to Israel failed, every promise standing and accomplished (the faithfulness complete, Joshua 23:14) — the nothing-failed (of any good thing).

On "all came to pass," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the whole fulfilled. Calvin and Gill note that every good thing came to pass, the whole accomplished, the faithfulness of God the ground of all assurance, fulfilled in Christ in whom all the promises are yea and amen (the sure word, Psalm 33:4; 2 Corinthians 1:20) — the figure (the certainty of every promise of God fulfilled in Christ); the providence (the faithfulness, the altar of witness by the eastern tribes to follow, ch.22). The verse displays that not one good thing failed, but all came to pass.

The witnesses agree the substance: not one of God's good promises to Israel failed, but every one was fulfilled (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's perfect faithfulness: not one good thing failed — the absolute and perfect faithfulness of God, who fulfilled to the letter every good word he had spoken, not one promise falling to the ground, the whole accomplished (Joshua 23:14), the faithfulness of God the ground of all assurance, his every promise sure (Psalm 33:4), a figure of the certainty of every promise of God fulfilled in Christ in whom all are yea and amen (2 Corinthians 1:20), the past faithfulness the pledge of the future. The verse displays the perfect-faithfulness crux. That not one good thing failed, but all came to pass, displays the crux: there failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass (Joshua 23:14; Psalm 33:4; 2 Corinthians 1:20) — the perfect faithfulness of God, every word of his promise fulfilled, not one falling to the ground, a figure of the certainty of every promise of God fulfilled in Christ in whom all are yea and amen. The verse closes the division of the land, before the eastern tribes return and build the altar of witness (ch.22).
